segments.text: Hi and welcome to this Sonar Summit
session with Dr. Cat Hicks. Dr. Hicks is
somebody I've known online for a while,
a psychological scientist whose research
focus is on how people learn and
particularly on how on how software
teams learn, which is of course could
not be more timely and a podcaster and
the author of a forthcoming book which
we'll be talking about soon. Did I
miss any of the key highlights there,
Cat?
Oh, love it. You know, something I like
to describe myself as is a research
architect. So, I like to put research
answers to questions that everybody's
asking and nobody's answering. So a
bit of a a a Robin Hood of grabbing data
and sharing it with the industry as some
of my friends have said. Yeah, but those
are the key points.
I mean, that is so timely both for
everybody who's gathered here for the
summit but also of course just for
the whole industry right now, right? I
mean that you've we
we're talking a lot about AI and how
it's impacting our teams, but I'm not
sure that we're really researching
what's going on to our teams right now.
I mean, can you talk a little bit about
where you feel like you fit in and the
and the work you're doing in this area?
Yeah, for sure. You know, I feel like
this into the agentic coding era, the AI
era, it is a time of a million tiny
experiments for every developer that I
talk to and also even people who have
been adjacent to technical work but have
never quite, you know, intersected with
code in the ways that they're doing now.
So, as a researcher, I see this
explosion in incredibly bespoke
experimentation. And you know, the
reason that that makes me really really
excited even though it's messy, it's
hard for me to sit here and say, "Hey, I
I can't tell you exactly one single
sweeping thing, you know, is going to be
the rule for everything." However, I do
know that the reason technology
innovation, you know, works at all is
really because of these kind of
collective collaborative, you know,
cumulative cultures where people get
together and they experiment and then we
uplevel individual experiments to the
group level and we do know some things
about that. So, I'm very very excited
about the fact that to me the stuff I've
always cared a lot about feels
incredibly important. And it also feels
like there is just room and space in
people's minds a little bit to start to
conceptualize
maybe like skills that I didn't know
were actually very important are
going to come out into the light now and
are going to actually help me be
successful in this new era because the
tools are different and the work is
different. And I think what we're going
to see succeed is a really interesting
empirical question.
Oh yeah, that that is empirical and
well there's going to be a lot of
proof in the pudding as well.
And personal questions.
Yeah, personal. I mean that's for sure.
I mean the you talked to you mentioned
there culture which I know is a key word
recurring word in a lot of the work you
do. Do you want to expand on that a
little bit because I think we're so used
to hearing that in our field as culture
in the sort of HR sense.
And you're using it I mean a little bit
in that sense but a little bit in in
other senses as well.
You know I mean I think that if you sit
down and talk to a developer you could
quickly find a million different
examples of times that they have entered
a culture or cultures they might feel
they belong to. You probably experienced
this yourself, right? Like there's
lawyer culture. There's, you know, the
culture of your individual company,
which is often how we hear it or we hear
that word is kind of like, okay, this is
the people stuff. And I'll work with,
you know, clients or engineering leaders
who say, okay, like culture, so that's
the stuff we're going to get to once we
solve the hard technical things. But for
me as a social scientist, like you don't
really have a choice. We are all
participating in cultures all the time.
So an example of this is something that
I measure in my research is what I call
learning culture. So do developers and
software teams feel like on this team
they are supported to learn and if
there's a moment where they need to take
time to learn do are they able to
have that conversation with their
manager? Are they able to share goals
with their teammates? You can actually
capture these kind of signals of the big
culture from people. When we do this
at a large scale like across thousands
of developers and we did it across more
than 12 industries, you find that
culture measures predict how productive
people say they're able to be. They also
predict how effective they say that
their teams are. So these are in some
ways like very intangible, hard to
measure. It's all around us
all the time. And we have to take
little slices of it, but in other ways
they're incredibly robust, real
connections that we're all responding to
all the time. So, I think it's very
important for technical leaders and
technical organizations to stop treating
this as a an add-on or a supplemental
thing. And take really seriously the
evidence that we have about how core
getting those psychological mechanisms or
psychological affordances kind of like a
design affordance. They're the
things that let you decide I can act
this way in this environment. It's safe
for me to do that. And when we see teams
really succeed at that, I mean, it kind
of helps you understand why did I
remember that one little scrappy team
where everyone was just really
supporting each other and for some
reason we were able to punch way above
our weight class, right? Even though we
didn't have the same resources and then
you could also probably remember like,
oh, I remember a big company and we had
all these resources, but we were bogged
down like we couldn't innovate because
some of the culture pieces were missing,
right? So I that's my whole realm.
That's my whole jam and it's what I
think really helps teams in this moment.
Yeah. I mean if that sounded
interesting to anybody listening today,
you should absolutely go find Cat's
podcast. We'll have some links to that
I'm sure on the website.
Thank you.
the learning culture
stuff I think is particularly I've been
familiar with your work for a while, but
I remember seeing you talk a little
bit about the learning culture stuff
because we're in this moment.
I mean I this might be
overgeneralization and push back if you
if you disagree but my sense is that a
lot of times the industry pays a little
bit of lip service to well of course
you're learning of course you're
figuring out the new API or figuring out
the new revision of the tool the new UX
or whatever but obviously right now we
are all industrywide all learning
together as fast and as hard as we can.
So love your like are there particular
indicators of learning culture that you
look for? And I know there's no one
magic bullet. I wish
there was but like what are the kinds of
things that you think of as either as
like healthy signs for a learning
culture or real red flags?
Yeah, great question. And I will say
yes, no silver bullets as you know I
have a paper that's called that. But you
I had forgotten that but I'm glad we go
for it.
That's right. And it's in reference to
famous Brook's work on software
engineering where he says there's no
silver bullet for software engineering.
But I will say boy if you measure at
least a couple things you'll start to
see really useful signals arise
and so I'm actually very optimistic
about this and have found that it's it's
pretty easy to start to pick up on this
and so indicators of learning culture.
So you you said a number of things that
I want to tap into here. So let me just
kind of organize my thoughts. Yeah.
Because one thing you said is we have
this sort of hypocrisy around learning
and I think that's really really
important
to think about. Another thing you
said was you know we kind of take it for
granted that it's just automatically
happening for software teams. And I
think that's to the credit of developers
who are like lifelong learners trying
really hard but I have seen in my
research a lot is put on the shoulders
of individuals and individuals are not
greater or bigger than their
environments. And so for a lot of
leaders, it is actually a much more
powerful lever for you to pull. Like you
get more bang for your buck if you can
improve, make some of these learning
moments much easier to access for your
people. And that can be easier than you
think. So some examples in our research,
we measure questions like, do
you feel like you can actually share
that you have a learning goal with your
team? That's one surface level signal,
but it picks up on a lot of whether
people feel like in this culture, am I
expected to know everything and I have
to hide when I don't know something to
look like a smart, brilliant developer,
or is this a place where we
have a little bit of a better mental
model coming from our leaders and our,
you know, senior engineers that is
expecting us to have learning
challenges. And this is one of the
reasons that you see these
big effects in research from the
presence of psychological safety. You
know, one of the things that we're
always doing is we're scanning around us
and we're asking, okay, maybe I care
about learning, but does everyone else
care about learning? And what are the
things that it's a permissible to
question here? And one of the things
that we picked up on in my research, the
study that I did called the AI skill
threat study or we called it the new
developer study, we looked at people who
felt like you have to be born a genius
to succeed at software engineering. And
this is kind of a stereotype that we
have. It's really really not borne out
in the evidence. It's not really true,
but it's such a myth that we have in
technical worlds. And the deeper you
internalize that, the harder it is for
you to pivot if suddenly being a
software engineer starts to look
different. So what's interesting though
is like very small cues can actually
help people
like I want to say deactivate. There's
no opposite of activate unactivate that
kind of set of beliefs. And some of
those cues are like people around you
starting to share actually I got to
where I am because I had to overcome
some learning and there was a time when
you know I didn't look like the classic
engineer stereotype and I really see
that in you. So all of these kind of
like psychological factors really steer
people towards believing that software
engineering could be something different
and I think that we have that present in
technical cultures like we have this
these beautiful values that I see teams
lean into like anybody could teach
themselves to code anybody could come
and innovate and if your work is good
enough it you know it could rise to the
top like that to me is a really
beautiful value but it's up against kind
of like fighting this war with these
other set of stereotypes that are like
you have to be born good at it and and
look amazing and ne and and and act like
you can grind all night and produce all
this code, you know, and that's what
makes you a good engineer. And I think
what is really really cool about this
moment is that whole story has just been
punctured like a balloon by AI because
it you can't really boast about how much
code you generate then like the robot is
generating all the code, right? So like
one of the fun things about that is for
psychologists like me, we look at
moments where people are experiencing a
lot of uncertainty and we think, "Oh my
gosh, that is a moment where you are
open to changing your mind about who you
could be in a way that you're often you
haven't been open to before." So I'm
excited about about all of that. There's
many pieces there we could dive into.
I well you know one thing I mean I think
a lot of our audience is people who are
forward thinking about this kind of thing but are
and some are leaders or some would like to be leaders
are there kinds of behaviors you think
that in these learning cultures that
you're talking about
are there behaviors that leaders
should be modeling
I mean you mentioned you mentioned
simply talking about learning and having
learning goals. I wonder if something as
simple as that for leaders talking
talking openly about their learning
experience with AI. Do you think that
would help?
I think that would absolutely help. But
you know what? I want to challenge that
slightly and kind of flip it around and
say, you know what I think leaders
should do is solicit this from their
people and say,
what do you think? You know, and
I know there are all kinds of barriers
to us being honest with each other in
any business. You know, I mean, we're we
all want to look good in front of each
other. We all want to like be pulling
together. You know, it's hard to answer
when your boss says something, but I am
a huge huge believer in the idea that
organizations can work to have a more
scientific mindset about themselves and
the people who are closest to these
problems. you know, the people who are
living it every day, they have things to
tell you about what is getting in their
way of learning. And those things can be
on the organizational level, they could
also be on the individual level. You
know, I've heard plenty of stories of
folks who said, "Well, you know, my
manager actually wanted us to take a
Friday learning day, but we none of us
really believed that that was an okay
thing to do." And you can get this
dynamic that we talk about in psychology
called pluralistic ignorance, which is a
like fascinating concept if you've never
heard it. It's when a whole group of
people all want to do something, but
they all think no one else wants it. So
they all like hold it back. And this is
not uncommon. This is like a very common
thing to happen in groups. So one of the
reasons I'm always saying bring it to
the surface. start to have the
conversation because you might actually
uncover that you all want to move
something. But it, you know, to the
hypocrisy point you brought up before, a
lot of people have experienced what I
like to call the poster on the wall
problem, which is like if you've ever
seen growth mindset poster, something
that says everyone can learn here and we
love learning and then you go back to
your manager and they say, well, you
shouldn't have learned like that, you
know, you shouldn't have taken that
extra day, right? So I tell leaders you
have to really really pay attention to
the signals you are sending about what
you are willing to prioritize and when.
And of course it's a balance but I see
the most successful leaders take risks
and saying I am going to really make a
move to show you you can push a deadline
if you have a learning goal or I will I
will make learning part of our
performance evaluation or have I ever
elevated someone in this org and given
them like a you know one of those
shoutouts or a bonus or something like
that because they mentored and taught
other people. All of those things are
like signals you can are kind of sending
to your organization about whether you
value this or not. And then of course,
as you know, Lewis, I've also been
really interested in the individual
level and like what developers can do to
sort of invest in cycles of learning
themselves more efficiently. So there's
an individual side to that, but that's
kind of where I would start with the
leader side.
Yeah. Well, let's absolutely dive into
that that individual side. But I I I
just wanted to say though, I think
maybe one of the reasons that I'm
optimistic about this industry in
particular,
you know, I think you and I have both
faced some push back about being more
positive about AI than a lot of other
people.
Yeah. Yeah.
this industry at least my sense is it's
less often hypocrisy and more just we're
all overloaded and
whereas in you know maybe in some other
industries that hypocrisy word is a
better label right I get the sense that often for a lot of folks
in tech it's just sort of like well of
course you have time to learn
and it and then
you're not thinking about it in that way
right I mean maybe with the individuals.
I definitely want to dig into it. Maybe
this is a good place for you to tell
what you've been experimenting with
lately because I think it's just
think it's just awesome.
Thank you. Yeah. So, you know, people
have misconceptions about learning and
how learning works. And you know, one of
those things that you're pointing out is
one of those misconceptions like, hey,
learning is going to happen
automatically and it's kind of going to
happen magically. I don't know when
it's going to happen, but it's going to
happen. and you know you're if you say
oh I don't know when it's going to
happen but it'll happen like that's like
nights and weekends you know that's
that's what's going to happen. I talk
in my book about this duality that we
face as people in wanting to have
performance goals like demonstrating
that you're competent to other people
and proving your comp and showing it and
sort of the outcome oriented goals and
in the research we we distinguish
that from mastery goals. So if you think
about, oh, I love something for what it
is and I'm working on the craft and I I
take joy in doing it, like kind of doing
it and the outcome becomes sort of one.
That's sort of why we call it intrinsic
motivation because it's like
intrinsically all there together.
we need both things in our
lives, but I see a lot of tech
organizations think the only thing I
should focus on is encouraging
performance. like we're a performance
culture, we're a high performing org.
They say all of these kinds of things. I
would urge people to pay attention to
where mastery lives in your environment
as well. So when I thought about trying
to help developers do this, I decided to
write a claude skill that actually pulls
from a bunch of nerdy excellent science
on helping people learn to be more
efficient learners, which is a excellent
thing to learn. It is actually a skill
itself like a human skill because we
tend to believe a lot of misconceptions.
So for instance we think that the more
we cra the more we're going to retain
information. We think that we should
passively read things a lot and that's
like exposing yourself to a lot of
information and it feels like you're
learning a lot. But actually, you know,
we see across lots of learning science,
you learn way better if you test
yourself. If you start to immediately
force yourself to retrieve information
from your mind, you might think about
like when you were in college and you
might have learned this as you realized
you were reading the same stuff over and
over again and it wasn't sinking in and
that you had to like go to flashcards or
quizzes or something like that. So, in
my Claude skill, I very mischievously
was like, I want to turn Claude from a
passive, it's just going to generate
everything to me and turn it into like a
thing that demands that I generate and I
do things. So, it's like a self- testing
skill that walks you through the files
that perhaps you already have generated
and that you haven't gone back and read
necessarily. And so it creates a 10 to
15 minute learning opportunity I called
it. And another cool thing about this is
we have actually learned in edtech that
small like micro learning like 8 to 10
minutes does add up. Like you can
meaningfully achieve learning goals in
those small amounts of time. And if you
do it more frequently like that is often
something people will stick with versus
a whole Friday of you know learning
afternoon that you're only getting to
like once a month or something like
that. So that was part of my thinking as
well is we've got to embed this stuff in
your workflow in you know you're you're
working your coding then you take a
little for break you work for 45 minutes
you take your 15 minutes of learning
that kind of reflection cadence is also
incredibly good for your long-term
memory of what you're doing. Building
all of that into a skill was really
cool. I kind of can't believe how well
it works actually.
I mean, I just love the you know, I
I love
taking this optimistic, but part of what
I just love about it is the vibe of
there's so much out there of like, oh,
well, AI just makes it impossible for
you to learn. And that I mean, certainly
I suspect we've all had these moments
of like, wait, what did I do? I know
the AI did something for me.
So, I mean, that fear is not

when we have this always on assistance.
Yeah. And I think you can
Yeah. Yeah. Right. I share that optimism
and I I see the risks exactly what
you're talking about. Like one of the
reasons I wanted to write this thing was
just a a proof of concept. Like I now
have the ability to create these little
speculative, you know, interfaces. Like
what if it looked like this, you know,
what if you were actually quizzing
yourself? What if that was super it was
super easy to make it really relevant
and embedded in your own actual work?
Because we also know people learn best
when it's very situated in their life.
Like going off and watching a video
course is boring for a lot of people.
Even when you know it's what you should

now. So people aren't necessarily

you know, I was thinking about it this

Yeah. That's I mean it's funny

like when I was first getting into this

I felt like my answer of like just grab
the tool and do something do anything it
doesn't really matter like that felt
sort of trit but I guess maybe what I'm

it's that learning by doing there really
is something there to it
no it's profound like you know we learn
by doing. Yeah.
And in some ways it's never at
least for intellectual work, it's never

You know, unfortunately, I mean, as
as I suspect our listeners can
probably tell, we could probably talk

have the Cat and Lewis conference.
tell us a little bit. You mentioned
already a book. it it's not quite out
yet if I'm if I'm right but I but it's
it's it's to bed at least. So
congratulations on that. Tell us a

Thank you. It's called the psychology of
software teams. It is coming out this
year hopefully beginning of the
summer and you can sign up for my
newsletter which is fightforthehum.com
or you could follow me on social
media. I'll post a lot of book stuff. I
loved writing a book. I have always been
a writer and I've been producing
psychology and doing all this open
science for software teams for a while.
So translating it into a book was a
complete joy. You know, I'm here in my
office and I can actually see my big co
post-it note board, you know, and
even in the automated era, you know, I
still do some things writing by hand.
And I have this one post-it note that I
save from when I was first drafting the
outline of the book. And it says
developers are people and that is the
heart of the book that there is this
core reality that technical work is done
by human beings. We have not always
listened to that or psychologists have
not always actually done a good job of
reaching out to everybody in this world
and translating that for you. So, my
hope is that this book gives you some
really great foundational science that's
like in your corner, has your back about
why your needs matter. One of my
friends said, "I'm going to buy the hard
cover so I can hit people with it in
this industry if I need to to give you a
sense of the kind of enthusiasm, you
know." But other people have said, you
know, that it I I think my favorite
thing to hear as a psychologist is you
named something I always have felt that
I already knew, but I did not have the

this book tells the story of for

it's funny for me coming out of open
source where so much of development was

of course it's always human but yet the

of course it's now all based on open

And yet we haven't really looked

excited
to read your book when it comes out
because I it is
you know, I appreciate that you're

thing that we've all
Thank you.
amateur hour like finger. Oh, yes. There
are people in the room.
Yeah. Well, it's you know what it's hard

you.

fullText: Hi and welcome to this Sonar Summit session with Dr. Cat Hicks. Dr. Hicks is somebody I've known online for a while, a psychological scientist whose research focus is on how people learn and particularly on how on how software teams learn, which is of course could not be more timely and a podcaster and the author of a forthcoming book which we'll be talking about soon. Did I miss any of the key highlights there, Cat? Oh, love it. You know, something I like to describe myself as is a research architect. So, I like to put research answers to questions that everybody's asking and nobody's answering. So a bit of a a a Robin Hood of grabbing data and sharing it with the industry as some of my friends have said. Yeah, but those are the key points. I mean, that is so timely both for everybody who's gathered here for the summit but also of course just for the whole industry right now, right? I mean that you've we we're talking a lot about AI and how it's impacting our teams, but I'm not sure that we're really researching what's going on to our teams right now. I mean, can you talk a little bit about where you feel like you fit in and the and the work you're doing in this area? Yeah, for sure. You know, I feel like this into the agentic coding era, the AI era, it is a time of a million tiny experiments for every developer that I talk to and also even people who have been adjacent to technical work but have never quite, you know, intersected with code in the ways that they're doing now. So, as a researcher, I see this explosion in incredibly bespoke experimentation. And you know, the reason that that makes me really really excited even though it's messy, it's hard for me to sit here and say, "Hey, I I can't tell you exactly one single sweeping thing, you know, is going to be the rule for everything." However, I do know that the reason technology innovation, you know, works at all is really because of these kind of collective collaborative, you know, cumulative cultures where people get together and they experiment and then we uplevel individual experiments to the group level and we do know some things about that. So, I'm very very excited about the fact that to me the stuff I've always cared a lot about feels incredibly important. And it also feels like there is just room and space in people's minds a little bit to start to conceptualize maybe like skills that I didn't know were actually very important are going to come out into the light now and are going to actually help me be successful in this new era because the tools are different and the work is different. And I think what we're going to see succeed is a really interesting empirical question. Oh yeah, that that is empirical and well there's going to be a lot of proof in the pudding as well. And personal questions. Yeah, personal. I mean that's for sure. I mean the you talked to you mentioned there culture which I know is a key word recurring word in a lot of the work you do. Do you want to expand on that a little bit because I think we're so used to hearing that in our field as culture in the sort of HR sense.
